BMW's Fully-Electric Vehicle Orders Surge in Europe
data.description:
By Nina Kienle


BMW said orders for fully electric vehicles in Europe surged in the first quarter, driven by strong customer interest in its new BMW iX3 model.

The German automaker on Tuesday said it has recorded well over 50,000 new orders for the BMW iX3 since ordering opened in Europe.

New battery-electric vehicle orders in Europe rose by around 40% on year in the first quarter, it said.

Across all types of vehicle, sales grew 11% in Germany.


Write to Nina Kienle at nina.kienle@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

04-14-26 0553ET

data.title: BHP Said Secures Some Easing to Chinese Iron Ore Buyer's Cargoes Ban
Fed nominee Warsh files financial disclosure in step towards confirmation
data.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - Kevin Warsh, the former Federal Reserve governor chosen by President Donald Trump to run the central bank, has submitted financial discloses that are required for his nomination to advance through the Senate, beginning with a yet-to-be-scheduled hearing.

Warsh's 69-page disclosure was filed overnight with the U.S. Office of Government Ethics, detailing his income and holdings including two investments listed as worth more than $50 million each in the Juggernaurt Fund LP, and $10,200,000 in consulting fees from the investment office of Wall Street giant Stanley Druckenmiller.

(Reporting by Howard Schneider; Editing by Andrew Heavens)

By Howard Schneider

Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Enjoys a Global Dose of Success
data.description:
The "Pharmacy of the World" flexed some serious muscle in 2025. The Indian pharma industry hit a record turnover of 4.72 trillion Indian Rupee—roughly USD 50.5bn.

For the first time ever, exports landed at USD 30.47bn, up 9.4% from the previous year (USD 27.85bn), as per India’s Ministry of Commerce and Industry. The US remains the biggest client, gobbling up nearly USD 9bn in exports, a massive 14.29% share.

With the global market projected to balloon from USD 1.6tn to USD 2.3tn by 2030 as per a report by Grand View Research, India is quickly moving past just being a budget supplier.

Leading that charge is Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Ltd, a domestic titan that’s been busy reinventing itself. The company has spent the year pivoting into a global specialty leader, setting a breakneck pace for what the future of Indian healthcare looks like. This shift has translated into double-digit profit growth for the fiscal year.

Bottom line glow-up

Sun Pharma pulled in a total revenue of INR 520.4bn—a 9% jump from the INR 477.6bn they saw in FY 24. The bottom line looked even better, with adjusted net profit hitting INR 119.8bn, a 19% y/y increase. This momentum was fueled by their Global Specialty business, which is becoming a major engine for them.

Their home turf in India is still the winner, making up 33% of the total business. Sales there hit INR169.2bn, growing 13.7% y/y compared to last year’s INR 148.9bn. The US business contributed 31% of the mix, with sales of INR 162.4bn, making it a steady 3.6% y/y climb.

The company’s medication, Ilumya, used to treat adults with moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis raked in USD 681m in global sales for FY 25. Meanwhile, the Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient and other income segment grew 11% y/y to INR 21.29bn.

A (growth) potion

Sun Pharma put their money where their mouth is with R&D in FY 25. The company poured in INR 32.48bn into the lab. That’s a steady 2.2% increase over the INR 31.78bn they spent back in FY 24.

About 40% of that entire R&D budget was dedicated towards their Global Specialty and innovation pipeline. Sun Pharma currently has 116 generic filings (ANDAs) and 14 new drug applications (NDAs) pending with the US FDA waiting for the green light. Despite this heavy investment in future innovation, the company's recent stock performance has seen a slight cooling off from its all-time highs.

The upside?

Sitting at INR 1,652.20, the price is actually down about 3.1% over the last 12 months, drifting away from its 52-week peak of INR 1,851.20. Even though the market cap is a staggering INR 3.9tn (USD 42.4bn), the valuation feels surprisingly chill.

With an average target price of INR 1,956.28, there’s an 18% upside on the table, some analysts think it could skyrocket 44% to hit INR 2,392.00. Trading at 34.1x, its projected FY 26 earnings, it’s slightly cheaper than its three-year average of 35.5x.

Bonus: the stock market pros are bullish. Out of 36 analysts tracking the stock, a massive 32 have voted for "Buy."

Side effects may include

The new 26% US reciprocal tariff is a major headache. Since Sun Pharma leans hard on patented specialty drugs such as Ilumya, they’re way more exposed to these costs than peers who just stick to generics. There’s massive buzz about a potential USD 12bn deal for US-based Organon & Co., though the company has officially labeled these reports as “speculative”. Taking on that kind of debt—plus Organon & Co’s USD 8.9bn baggage—could flip Sun's conservative, cash-positive reputation upside down.

Throw in the never-ending USFDA compliance headaches at manufacturing plants, and you’ve got a potentially bumpy ride.

HSBC's Europe and Americas banking head Keefe departs
data.description:
HONG KONG, April 14 (Reuters) - HSBC's head of banking in Europe and the Americas Gerry Keefe has resigned, becoming the latest senior banker to depart since the bank's global overhaul launched in 2024.

The bank announced the news on Tuesday, according to an internal memo reviewed by Reuters and confirmed by a company spokesperson.

Jo Miyake, head of Asia and the Middle East banking, was named to take on Keefe's role on an interim basis, the bank spokesperson said. 

Keefe was appointed to the role of overseeing the bank's commercial and investment banking operations in the two regions about a year ago.

HSBC in a major restructuring to pivot to Asia, wound down its mergers and acquisition, and some equities businesses in the Americas and Europe in early 2025.

Keefe, who joined HSBC from Citigroup in 2021 as head of banking for Americas, was among the high-profile bankers the European bank hired to try to boost its U.S. dealmaking.

Last September, HSBC's former head of U.S. banking Lisa McGeough left to join rival Deutsche Bank. 

(Reporting by Selena Li; editing by Barbara Lewis)

By Selena Li

data.title: Shell, in contrast to BP, allows AGM vote on Follow This climate resolution
data.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Shell will allow its shareholders to vote on a resolution tabled by climate activist investor group Follow This at its annual general meeting, recommending a vote against it, according to its notice of meeting.

This stands in contrast to BP, whose board decided not to include the Follow This resolution on its agenda, triggering some of its shareholders and influential proxy adviser groups to recommend votes against the board's wishes. 

(Reporting by Shadia Nasralla; Editing by Kirsten Donovan)
